title: Of War and Men By Ralph LaRossa University of Chicago Press. 2011. 328 pages. $32 paper
abstract: In Of War and Men, Ralph LaRossa details the shifting terrain of fatherhood during World War II and its aftermath through 1960. In this work, LaRossa picks up where his last major work, The Modernization of Fatherhood: A Social and Political History, left off. Acknowledging his prior work is relevant because within that work, he argued that the modernizing trends in fatherhood through the 1930s were generally progressive, emphasizing fatherhood involvement with children. With the beginning of World War II, the modernizing trend is halted and eventually reversed across the 1950s in ways connected to the war. To arrive at this conclusion, LaRossa distinguishes between the culture of fatherhood and the conduct of fatherhood. Separating cultural ideas and ideals from men's behaviors is always important, but it is especially so for the era he covers because so much of our contemporary popular and academic discourse about fatherhood in the 1950s tends to focus on the era's culture of fatherhood to the exclusion of the actual practices of men at the time. It is also important because understandings of contemporary fatherhood are often contrasted with our (mis)understandings of fathers from the 1950s.
